interaction_phrase: senior staff in the Quantum Information and Integrated Nanosystems Group
occurrence_sentence: "What kind of quantum information processing architecture is feasible for trapped ions? If it turns out it will be much more difficult to use a certain ion species, it would be important to know early on, before you head far down that path," says John Chiaverini, senior staff in the Quantum Information and Integrated Nanosystems Group.
Toward answering these questions, MIT Lincoln Laboratory researchers have turned to a promising pair: ions of calcium (Ca) and strontium (Sr).

interaction_phrase: senior staff in the Quantum Information and Integrated Nanosystems Group
occurrence_sentence: "What kind of quantum information processing architecture is feasible for trapped ions? If it turns out it will be much more difficult to use a certain ion species, it would be important to know early on, before you head far down that path," says John Chiaverini, senior staff in the Quantum Information and Integrated Nanosystems Group.
"When two qubits are entangled, their states are dependent on each other. They are so-called 'spookily correlated,'" Chiaverini said.
Lincoln Laboratory researchers have been working for the past several years on a way deliver the lasers up through "gratings" in the chip the ions hover above.

"It's much simpler working with this light than the ultraviolet, especially when you start to put a lot of these ions together. But that's the challenge—no one actually knows what kind of architecture will enable quantum computation that's helpful. The jury is still out," Chiaverini reflects.
